title: Getting Started with Protocols.io at UCSB
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"50doo","text":"Protocols.io is an open-access, collaborative platform for creating, managing, and sharing step-by-step research methods/protocols in a dynamic, interactive format. It can be useful for researchers in any discipline who are interested in:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"57ju8","text":"Creating a workspace for your lab to organize and version protocols","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5pl3r","text":"Forking (i.e., duplicate and modify) existing protocols to adapt them for your project","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":9,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3ciau","text":"Embedding media, such as images and videos, to clarify steps","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7jbq3","text":"Publishing detailed experimental methods with a DOI that can be linked in publications","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aafb0","text":"Running protocols as interactive checklists on the web or mobile devices","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4q8hd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1nr3u","text":"UCSB Library is pleased to provide faculty, staff, and students with premium access to protocols.io. The following instructions walk you through setting up an account and accessing premium features.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"fl08q","text":"If you’d like to create a new protocols.io account using your UCSB credentials, follow steps 1-3.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7qi55","text":"If you have an existing protocols.io account (e.g., from a prior institution, third-party login, etc.) and would like to take advantage of UCSB’s premium license, start with step 4.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":46,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":97,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Go to protocols.io and click on the “Sign Up” button in the upper right corner. Screenshot of protocols.io homepage with "Sign Up" button

From the “Create an account” page, click on “Continue with SSO” and enter your UCSB email on the next page. You will be prompted to log in with your UCSB netID and password. You may be asked to verify your identity through DUO MFA Security. Screenshots showing how to create a protocols.io account with SSO ‱ Always double-check the URL in your browser’s address bar before entering your UCSB credentials. It should begin with https://sso.ucsb.edu. If it does not, the site is not legitimate, and you should stop immediately. Alternative sign-in option 1. Create a unique protocols.io account using your UCSB email address and a separate password. You may be asked to confirm your email by entering a verification code. Screenshots showing how to create a unique login for protocols.io using your UCSB email address Enter your name, affiliation, and job title (optional). Your full name will be publicly visible on your protocols. Checking the “Private profile” box will hide your profile from public searches. Your profile will only be visible to members of workspaces you belong to. Screenshot of webpage for entering your name, affiliation, and job title Alternative sign-in option 2. Use a third-party login. “Continue with Springer Nature”: Link your existing Springer Nature account. “Continue with ORCID”: Sign up with your ORCID to automatically link your published content to your ORCID profile. “Continue with Google”: Use your UCSB-issued Google account to automatically access UCSB’s premium license. ‱ If you choose to use a third-party login, you will be automatically logged into that platform ( e.g. , ORCID, Google) in addition to protocols.io. When you complete your work (especially on a shared computer), remember to log out of BOTH protocols.io and the third-party app to ensure your privacy and data security . This might entail going to the third-party website ( e.g. , orcid.org or google.com ) and logging out separately.
Create your first workspace. Name your workspace. Invite colleagues to your workspace (optional). You can always invite collaborators later. Screenshot of webpage for creating your first workspace

Check that your workspace is linked to the UCSB premium license: There should be a “PREMIUM” tag next to your workspace name There should be a blue “UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA” logo in the menu bar. When expanded, the menu bar should say UC Santa Barbara Screenshots showing how to check that your workspace is linked to the UCSB premium license

[Troubleshooting] If you do not see the UCSB premium license or if you’d like to link an existing protocols.io account to the UCSB premium license: Click on your profile picture in the upper right corner. In the dropdown menu, click “Go to Profile” Go to “Edit profile” Make sure your UCSB email address is listed under the “Contact information” section Use the “Save changes” button in the upper-right corner of the page to save any updates Screenshots showing how to update contact info for an existing account If you’d like to use a non-UCSB email address for your protocols.io account, you can still take advantage of the UCSB premium license by verifying your affiliation: Click on the “Upgrade” button Select UC Santa Barbara as your organization Verify your organization account by entering your UCSB email address. A verification email will be sent to you. Follow the steps there. Screenshot showing how to upgrade your account by confirming your UCSB affiliation If you have previously signed up or logged in to protocols.io using a third-party login ( e.g. , ORCID, Google) and would like to create a separate and unique login for protocols.io, you may do so by going to "Edit profile", updating your contact information, and creating a password. When logging in in the future, you’d be able to use this new login. Screenshot showing how to create a unique protocols.io login/password for your existing account ‱ If you are still encountering issues, email rds@library.ucsb.edu (for UCSB-specific questions) or info@protocols.io (for general protocols.io questions).

With the UCSB premium license, you’ll be able to create unlimited private protocols and more . Here are some references and resources to get you started on your protocol writing/sharing journey. Protocols.io Help Center. https://www.protocols.io/help/getting-started Protocols.io Short Video Tutorials. https://www.protocols.io/tutorials Baker M. 2021. Five keys to writing a reproducible lab protocol. Nature 597:293–294. doi: 10.1038/d41586-021-02428-3 Borm L. 2023. Methods section too short? Use online protocols to make complex techniques understandable. Nature. doi: 10.1038/d41586-023-03249-2 Ganley E, Broellochs A, Teytelman L. 2021. What to include in your protocol. protocols.io doi: 10.17504/protocols.io.bvkfn4tn
creator: Jade Li
